title: Fact-Checking GPT-4 Outputs
summary: Atomic fact verification on ChatGPT biographies using TF-IDF retrieval and DeBERTa-MNLI entailment. 85.5% accuracy.
subtitle: A FActScore-style pipeline that decomposes ChatGPT-generated biographies into atomic facts and verifies each one against retrieved Wikipedia passages - with an error analysis that surfaces a recurring failure mode I call cross-abstractive alignment.

cardImage.alt: 2x2 matrix categorizing fact-checking errors by abstraction level

heroImage.alt: Cross-abstractive alignment matrix: fact vs. support compression axes with example facts in each cell

sections.title: Overview
sections.body:
Large language models like GPT-4 fluently produce biographical text that looks right but quietly hallucinates - a sentence about a real person can mix supported claims, unsupported-but-plausible claims, and outright fabrications. This project tackles that problem at the level of atomic facts: given a single short factual claim extracted from a ChatGPT-generated biography and a small set of retrieved Wikipedia passages, decide whether the passages support the fact (S) or not (NS).

I built and compared two systems - a lexical TF-IDF baseline and a natural-language-inference (NLI) entailment classifier - on a human-labeled dataset of ChatGPT atomic facts paired with BM25-retrieved Wikipedia passages. The final entailment system reaches 85.5% accuracy with balanced per-class F1 (S: 0.852, NS: 0.858), and the more interesting output is the error analysis: a categorization of failure modes that points toward what a stronger fact-checker would actually need to do.

sections.title: The task
sections.body: The dataset, derived from the FActScore setup, gives you a single sentence-sized fact (e.g. "Marianne McAndrew is a singer."), a list of retrieved Wikipedia passages, and a gold label of S, NS, or IR (irrelevant). IR is folded into NS for evaluation, so the predictor outputs a binary S/NS decision per fact. The interesting failure cases are facts that look topically related to the passage but aren't actually entailed by it - exactly the kind of mistake a human skim-reader would also miss.

sections.title: Pipeline
sections.alt: Four-stage pipeline: passage windowing, top-k pruning by composite score, DeBERTa-MNLI entailment, threshold + win-gap decision
sections.caption: The entailment pipeline. Sliding windows over each passage are scored cheaply, the top k are passed through DeBERTa-MNLI, and S is returned only when entailment beats both an absolute threshold and a margin over neutral/contradiction.

sections.title: Approach
sections.intro: Both systems share a windowing step - passages are split into sentences and adjacent bi-sentence pairs, normalized, and lemmatized via spaCy. They diverge in how they score those windows against the candidate fact:
items: Word-recall (TF-IDF) baseline. Build a sparse TF-IDF vector for the fact and each window using IDF weights computed over the windows themselves. Predict S if any window beats both a recall threshold (lemmas of the fact covered by the window) and a cosine threshold, with a small overlap floor to suppress trivial matches.
Entailment system. Use MoritzLaurer/DeBERTa-v3-base-mnli-fever-anli as a premise → hypothesis NLI classifier, treating each window as the premise and the fact as the hypothesis.

Top-k pruning before NLI. Running NLI over every window is slow and noisy. Each window gets a cheap composite score - 0.6 x recall + 0.3 x Jaccard + 0.1 x bigram-overlap - and only the top 24 are sent through DeBERTa. This both speeds up inference and removes obvious distractors before they can bait the entailment model into a wrong call.

Decision rule. Predict S only if entailment probability ≥ 0.55 and the win gap (entailment - max(neutral, contradiction)) ≥ 0.15. The gap matters: a fact getting 0.55 entail / 0.46 neutral is a coin flip dressed up as confidence.

sections.title: Results
sections.body:
On the dev set of 221 facts, the entailment system reaches 85.5% accuracy - TP 92, TN 97, FP 12, FN 20. Per-class F1 is balanced (0.852 for S, 0.858 for NS), so the model isn't winning by collapsing onto one class.

The headline number undersells the more interesting structure in the errors: all 20 false negatives were caused by entailment falling below the absolute threshold, not by the win gap. In most of those, predicted entailment wasn't borderline - it was very low (often < 0.05). The model wasn't uncertain on the misses; it was confidently wrong. False positives, by contrast, split roughly in half between high-entailment confident wrongs and threshold-skirting marginal calls.

image.alt: Confusion matrix and per-class metrics: accuracy 0.855, F1 for S 0.852, F1 for NS 0.858

sections.title: Error analysis
sections.body:
I sorted every false positive and false negative into one of three buckets:

Semantic summarization. The fact is correctly labeled and supported in the passage, but only after synthesizing several distant sentences. "Gerhard Fischer is best known for inventing a metal detector" is supported by a passage that talks about Fischer founding a research lab, patenting the device in 1937, and Einstein endorsing the concept - but no single window says "best known for." Recall, Jaccard, and bigram overlap all collapse, the window gets pruned, and the model never sees the right premise. These dominate the false negatives.

Detail mismatching. Truth hinges on a single token. "Itakura has represented Japan at the Under-23 level" is labeled NS because the passage actually says U-20, but DeBERTa fires S with entail=0.553, gap=0.140 - just barely over both thresholds. The lexical signal is overwhelming and the one disqualifying token gets averaged away.

Poor fact/support. The annotation itself is wrong, or the retrieved passage simply doesn't mention the relevant entity. "Cleveland is in Ohio" is labeled NS in the gold data; the model returns S with entail=0.994. Without a stronger retrieval step (or a real RAG pipeline that can fall back to outside knowledge), there's no obvious fix.

sections.title: Cross-abstractive alignment
sections.body:
Plotting the failure cases on two axes - does the fact need compression to a few salient tokens, or to a summarized concept? does the support? - produces a 2x2 with an example failure in each cell.

The pattern I'd call cross-abstractive alignment: an accurate fact-checker has to compress both the fact and the support to comparable levels of abstraction before comparing them. When fact and passage live at different abstraction levels - a token-pivot fact against a summarized passage, or vice versa - the entailment model's surface-level features stop carrying the signal that decides the label.

This reframes "why entailment fails" away from "the model isn't smart enough" and toward something more architectural: the comparison step needs to operate on representations that have already been aligned to the right granularity. That's a much more tractable problem to design around than "build a better NLI model."

image.alt: 2x2 matrix of error categories with example facts in each cell

sections.title: Takeaways
items: Lexical pre-filtering buys most of the wins, and most of the losses. Top-k pruning by recall + Jaccard + bigram overlap is what makes NLI tractable on this dataset, but it also throws away exactly the cases where the fact and the support sit at different abstraction levels - the very cases an entailment model is most needed for.

Threshold + win-gap is a meaningful upgrade over a single threshold. Roughly half the false positives in a single-threshold version came from cases where entailment narrowly beat neutral; requiring a margin removes those without sacrificing recall.

The hard cases are about abstraction, not about the NLI model. A bigger entailment model would probably help with detail-mismatching errors, but cross-abstractive cases need either retrieval that surfaces the right multi-sentence span or a summarization step that aligns fact and passage to the same level before comparison.
~10% of errors are dataset issues. Cleveland really is in Ohio. Some labels are noise, and a fact-checker that pretends otherwise will pay for it on the metrics.
